content.text: RAG (Red / Amber / Green) escalation rules define exactly when a status change must trigger a formal notification, what action is required, and who must be notified. Without these rules, RAG status becomes a decorative colour scheme rather than an operational signal. With them, it becomes an early-warning system with clear accountability chains.
What It Is

RAG escalation rules are a documented set of threshold conditions, time-to-escalate service level agreements (SLAs), escalation paths, required actions per status, and de-escalation criteria. They transform the subjective colour coding of a status report into an objective trigger mechanism. The rules specify: what conditions cause a dimension to move from green to amber or from amber to red; who must be notified within what timeframe; what artefacts must be produced (e.g., a mitigation plan); and what conditions allow a red or amber rating to be downgraded.

For GenAI programs, escalation rules must account for both project-level signals (schedule delay, budget overrun) and system-level signals (model accuracy degradation, data pipeline failure, safety classification failures). These two signal classes have different escalation paths and different resolution owners.
Why It Matters

Colour-based status without escalation rules degrades into two failure modes. In the first, program managers self-censor amber and red ratings to avoid difficult conversations, producing systematically optimistic reporting that misleads governance forums. In the second, every amber rating triggers the same frantic response regardless of severity, causing alert fatigue that desensitises stakeholders to genuine crises.

Explicit escalation rules solve both problems by making the criteria objective (reducing self-censorship) and the response proportionate (reducing alert fatigue). They also protect program managers: when escalation is rules-based, raising a red flag is a professional obligation rather than a personal judgment call.
Benefits
Reduces subjective RAG gaming — thresholds are documented, not left to personal judgment.
Ensures problems reach the right decision-maker before they become unrecoverable.
Creates proportionate responses — minor amber issues get attention without consuming executive bandwidth.
Provides a paper trail: escalations are time-stamped and visible in governance records.
Enables consistent behaviour across multiple programs in a portfolio — the same rule applies to all.
Protects program managers by making escalation a procedural obligation, not a personal choice.
Trade-offs
Over-prescriptive rules can create false red alerts — a one-day schedule slip triggering an exec escalation wastes goodwill.
Rules require periodic recalibration as program risk profile changes across lifecycle phases.
Enforcement depends on culture: rules are only as effective as the governance body's willingness to hold PMs accountable.
GenAI-specific thresholds (e.g., what constitutes an unacceptable hallucination rate) require domain expertise to calibrate correctly.
How It Works
Threshold Definitions

Each RAG dimension has three threshold states. Green means conditions are within acceptable range and no special action is needed. Amber means conditions are outside the acceptable range but the program can recover without immediate executive intervention if mitigation begins now. Red means conditions are outside the recoverable range without external intervention, resource reallocation, or scope change.

Thresholds must be specific and measurable. 'Schedule is at risk' is not a threshold; '4 or more working days behind the critical path baseline' is. For GenAI quality dimensions, a threshold might be 'hallucination rate exceeds 5% on three consecutive evaluation runs' rather than 'model is performing poorly.'
Time-to-Escalate SLAs

Escalation SLAs specify the maximum time between a threshold being crossed and the first notification being sent. Typical SLAs: Green-to-Amber transition — notify PM and sponsor within 24 hours; Amber-to-Red transition — notify exec sponsor and steering committee within 4 hours; Production incident (system down or safety failure) — notify CTO-level and program sponsor within 1 hour regardless of current RAG status.

SLA timers start from the point when the PM has sufficient information to confirm the threshold has been crossed, not from the time of the event itself. This distinction matters for complex situations like gradual model drift where the crossing point is not instantaneous.
Escalation Paths

Escalation paths define who is notified at each level. A typical three-tier path: Level 1 (Amber) — Program Manager notifies Executive Sponsor via email with status report attached; Level 2 (Red) — PM notifies Executive Sponsor and Steering Committee Chair with a formal issue notification and preliminary impact assessment; Level 3 (Critical incident) — PM notifies Executive Sponsor, CTO, and (if safety-related) Chief AI Officer with immediate verbal briefing followed by written summary within 2 hours.
Required Actions per Status

Each status level requires specific artefacts. Amber requires a written mitigation plan with actions, owners, and dates within 48 hours of the escalation notification. Red requires a written recovery plan with executive approval within 24 hours and a daily status update until the rating returns to amber. Production critical incident requires an incident commander designation, a status page update if customer-facing, and a post-incident review scheduled within 5 days of resolution.
De-escalation Criteria

De-escalation prevents ratings from being stuck in red indefinitely once remediation is underway. De-escalation from Red to Amber requires: the root cause to be identified; the mitigation plan approved; and the leading indicators to show improvement over two consecutive reporting periods. De-escalation from Amber to Green requires: all actions in the mitigation plan closed or explicitly accepted as risks; and the triggering metric to return within green threshold for two consecutive periods.
Architecture

attrs.alt: RAG escalation decision tree showing transitions from Green through Amber to Red with escalation actions and de-escalation path

content.text: When to Use It
At program inception: publish the escalation rules as part of the governance framework before the first status report.
When onboarding a new program manager who has not operated under this framework before.
When a governance forum notes inconsistent escalation behaviour across programs in the portfolio.
When a production GenAI incident reveals that the existing rules did not trigger the right response fast enough.
Annually as part of program governance review — thresholds may need recalibration as the program matures.
How to Implement
Draft threshold definitions for each RAG dimension with the program manager, tech lead, and executive sponsor present — consensus on what amber means prevents disputes later.
Document escalation SLAs in the program governance charter, not just the status report template.
Assign a named escalation recipient for each level and ensure backup contacts are defined for holidays and leaves.
Test the escalation path in week 1 with a dry run — send a test amber notification and confirm receipt and understanding.
Integrate escalation triggers with observability tooling where possible: a PagerDuty-style alert for production RAG thresholds reduces reliance on manual judgment.
Review escalation rules at each major phase transition (e.g., moving from build to production) — risk profiles change at phase boundaries.
Record every escalation event in the program risk log so patterns can be analysed at retrospectives.
Examples
Escalation Rules Reference Table

# GenAI Program RAG Escalation Rules v1.0
# Effective: 2026-07-19

## SCHEDULE DIMENSION
| Threshold        | RAG    | Action Required                    | SLA  | Notify            |
|------------------|--------|------------------------------------|------|-------------------|
| 0-1 day delay    | GREEN  | None                               | —    | —                 |
| 2-4 day delay    | AMBER  | Mitigation plan                    | 24h  | PM → Sponsor      |
| 5+ day delay     | RED    | Recovery plan + exec approval      | 4h   | PM → Exec + SC    |
| Milestone missed | RED    | Revised plan + sponsor signature   | 4h   | PM → Exec + SC    |

## BUDGET DIMENSION
| Threshold                    | RAG    | Action              | SLA  | Notify       |
|------------------------------|--------|---------------------|------|--------------|
| Within 5% of forecast        | GREEN  | None                | —    | —            |
| 5-10% overspend vs forecast  | AMBER  | Reforecast + plan   | 24h  | PM → Sponsor |
| >10% overspend vs forecast   | RED    | Reforecast + exec   | 4h   | PM → Exec    |
| Forecast to overrun by >20%  | RED    | Emergency review    | 4h   | PM → Exec    |

## MODEL QUALITY DIMENSION
| Threshold                          | RAG    | Action                    | SLA  | Notify             |
|------------------------------------|--------|---------------------------|------|--------------------|
| Hallucination rate < 3%            | GREEN  | None                      | —    | —                  |
| Hallucination rate 3-6%            | AMBER  | Root cause + retrain plan | 24h  | PM → ML Lead       |
| Hallucination rate > 6%            | RED    | Feature flag / rollback   | 1h   | PM → Exec + CTO    |
| Safety classification failure      | RED    | Immediate rollback        | 1h   | PM → Exec + CAO    |

## DATA DIMENSION
| Threshold                           | RAG    | Action                  | SLA  | Notify           |
|-------------------------------------|--------|-------------------------|------|------------------|
| Pipeline lag < 4h vs SLA            | GREEN  | None                    | —    | —                |
| Pipeline lag 4-24h                  | AMBER  | Root cause in 24h       | 24h  | PM → Data Lead   |
| Pipeline lag > 24h or data loss     | RED    | Incident response       | 1h   | PM → Exec + CTO  |
Python escalation check automation

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
import smtplib

class RAG(Enum):
    GREEN = "GREEN"
    AMBER = "AMBER"
    RED = "RED"

@dataclass
class EscalationRule:
    dimension: str
    rag: RAG
    sla_hours: float
    notify: list[str]
    required_action: str

ESCALATION_RULES = {
    ("schedule", RAG.AMBER): EscalationRule(
        dimension="schedule",
        rag=RAG.AMBER,
        sla_hours=24,
        notify=["pm@co.com", "sponsor@co.com"],
        required_action="Submit mitigation plan within 48h"
    ),
    ("schedule", RAG.RED): EscalationRule(
        dimension="schedule",
        rag=RAG.RED,
        sla_hours=4,
        notify=["exec@co.com", "steering@co.com"],
        required_action="Recovery plan with exec approval within 24h"
    ),
    ("model_quality", RAG.RED): EscalationRule(
        dimension="model_quality",
        rag=RAG.RED,
        sla_hours=1,
        notify=["cto@co.com", "exec@co.com"],
        required_action="Rollback decision within 1h"
    ),
}

def check_and_escalate(dimension: str, rag: RAG, detected_at: datetime):
    rule = ESCALATION_RULES.get((dimension, rag))
    if rule is None:
        return  # Green, no action
    deadline = detected_at + timedelta(hours=rule.sla_hours)
    print(f"ESCALATION REQUIRED: {dimension} is {rag.value}")
    print(f"Notify: {', '.join(rule.notify)}")
    print(f"Deadline: {deadline.isoformat()}")
    print(f"Required action: {rule.required_action}")

# Example
check_and_escalate("schedule", RAG.RED, datetime.utcnow())
Real-World Example

A healthcare GenAI program used RAG escalation rules with a specific threshold for clinical suggestion accuracy: if the model's recall on a validation set dropped below 92% for two consecutive weekly evaluations, the quality dimension moved from green to amber and the ML lead was required to submit a retraining plan within 48 hours. If recall dropped below 88%, the rating moved to red and the Chief Medical Officer was notified within 4 hours alongside a recommendation to suspend the model from active clinical use pending remediation. This explicit rule meant that the clinical governance team could rely on the process rather than depending on the program team's subjective judgment to surface patient safety risks.
Common Mistakes
Defining thresholds in absolute terms without accounting for program phase — a 3-day delay in week 1 is very different from a 3-day delay in week 48.
Conflating escalation and blame — escalation rules should be framed as information flows, not performance management triggers.
Omitting de-escalation criteria — programs can remain stuck in red indefinitely without a clear path back to amber.
Using the same SLAs for all incident types — a production GenAI safety failure needs a 1-hour response, not the same 24-hour SLA as a budget amber.
Not testing escalation paths — contact details go stale, distribution lists break, and backup contacts are undefined until tested.
Setting amber thresholds so loosely that everything is always green until catastrophic failure makes red unavoidable.
Best Practices
Publish escalation rules in the same document as the status report template — they must be read together.
Include numeric thresholds for every dimension rather than relying on qualitative descriptions.
Separate project escalation paths (schedule, budget) from system escalation paths (model quality, data, production incidents).
Name specific individuals and backup contacts, not just roles — roles are ambiguous when people change.
Review escalation rules at every major milestone review to ensure they remain appropriate for the program's current risk profile.
Log every escalation event so patterns can surface systemic governance problems at portfolio reviews.
Performance & Scalability

At portfolio scale, individual program escalation events need to roll up to a portfolio-level view. Implement a central escalation register where all program amber and red events are recorded with timestamps. A portfolio manager can then run weekly queries to identify programs with sustained amber ratings (a leading indicator of future reds) and programs with frequent escalation events (a leading indicator of governance dysfunction).

Automated threshold monitoring via observability platforms (Datadog, Grafana, Prometheus) can trigger escalation notifications directly for technical dimensions like model accuracy, latency, and data freshness, removing the dependency on a human program manager to notice and report the threshold crossing. This is particularly valuable for GenAI systems where degradation can be gradual rather than sudden.
Tooling & Ecosystem
PagerDuty / OpsGenie: on-call escalation paths for production system incidents, integrates with model monitoring.
Confluence / Notion: document the escalation rules and link them from each status report.
Jira / ServiceNow: create escalation records as tickets for traceability and audit.
Grafana Alerting: threshold-based alerts for model quality metrics (latency, accuracy, hallucination rate).
Slack / Teams with governance channels: define specific channels for amber and red notifications so the right people receive the right alerts.
Power Automate / Zapier: route threshold breaches from monitoring tools to governance notification channels automatically.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Strengths
Weaknesses
Best For
Explicit written escalation rules (this topic)
Objective, auditable, consistent
Requires calibration effort upfront
Formal programs with governance structures
Ad hoc PM judgment
Flexible, context-sensitive
Inconsistent, prone to optimism bias
Informal or very early-stage projects
Automated monitoring only
Fast, removes human delay
Misses non-technical signals (scope, decisions)
Production system alerting, not program governance
Executive calendar-only escalation
Simple, executives already engaged
Periodic, not responsive to sudden changes
Low-risk programs with stable profiles
Security & Reliability
Escalation notifications for Red status often contain sensitive information (financial exposure, regulatory risk) — route via secure channels, not open Slack channels.
Ensure escalation contact lists are kept current in a system of record, not ad hoc in individual PMs' heads.

For production GenAI incidents, integrate with your security incident response process — some model failures (prompt injection, data exfiltration via LLM) are security incidents requiring CISO involvement.
Test escalation channels quarterly; stale email distribution lists or misconfigured PagerDuty integrations mean escalations may silently fail.
Key Takeaways
RAG escalation rules transform subjective colour coding into an objective trigger mechanism with defined actions and accountability.
Each dimension needs specific numeric thresholds, not qualitative descriptions, to prevent gaming and inconsistency.
Time-to-escalate SLAs vary by severity: 24 hours for amber, 4 hours for red, 1 hour for production safety incidents.
De-escalation criteria are as important as escalation criteria — without them, programs remain stuck in red after resolution.
GenAI programs need separate escalation paths for technical signals (model quality, data) and project signals (schedule, budget).
Automated threshold monitoring removes human delay for technical dimensions and improves response time reliability.
Every escalation event should be logged; patterns across the portfolio reveal systemic governance problems.
